questions.stem: A data scientist wants to normalize feature values to the range [0, 1] using Snowpark ML before training. Which preprocessor should they use?

options.text: snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.StandardScaler
snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.MinMaxScaler
snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.RobustScaler
snowflake.ml.modeling.preprocessing.MaxAbsScaler

questions.explanation: B is correct: MinMaxScaler transforms features to a [0, 1] range by computing (x - min) / (max - min) for each value, running the computation inside Snowflake. A is wrong: StandardScaler standardizes to zero mean and unit variance, producing values that can be negative and are not bounded to [0, 1]. C is wrong: RobustScaler uses median and IQR for scaling, which is outlier-robust but does not produce [0, 1] range values. D is wrong: MaxAbsScaler scales by the maximum absolute value, producing [-1, 1] range for data with both positive and negative values.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML Preprocessing — MinMaxScaler

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to evaluate a Snowpark ML RandomForestClassifier using 5-fold cross-validation. Which code snippet is correct?

options.text:
from snowflake.ml.modeling.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(estimator=rf_model, X=train_df, y='LABEL', cv=5)

from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(rf_model, train_df.to_pandas(), train_df['LABEL'].to_pandas(), cv=5)

from snowflake.ml.modeling.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(rf_model, train_df, label_cols=['LABEL'], cv=5)
scores = rf_model.cross_validate(train_df, folds=5)

questions.explanation: C is correct: Snowpark ML's cross_val_score (from snowflake.ml.modeling.model_selection) accepts a fitted or unfitted Snowpark ML estimator, a Snowpark DataFrame, and uses label_cols to specify the target column. cv=5 specifies 5-fold cross-validation. A is wrong: the signature uses label_cols, not a separate y parameter. B is wrong: using sklearn's cross_val_score with pandas conversion defeats the purpose of Snowpark ML. D is wrong: cross_validate() is not a method on Snowpark ML estimator objects.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML Model Selection — cross_val_score

questions.stem: A team trains a Snowpark ML XGBClassifier and wants to explain feature importance. After calling `model.fit(train_df)`, how do they access feature importance scores?

options.text: model.feature_importances_ returns a Snowpark DataFrame with importance scores
Call model.to_sklearn().feature_importances_ to access the underlying XGBoost model's feature importance array
model.explain(train_df) generates a SHAP explanation DataFrame
Feature importance is not accessible from Snowpark ML models

questions.explanation: B is correct: Snowpark ML estimators expose a to_sklearn() method that returns the underlying fitted sklearn-compatible or XGBoost model. From there, standard attributes like feature_importances_ are accessible. A is wrong: Snowpark ML model objects do not expose a feature_importances_ attribute directly as a Snowpark DataFrame. C is wrong: SHAP explanation is not a built-in method on Snowpark ML models (though you could use the underlying model with the shap library). D is wrong: feature importance is accessible via to_sklearn().
questions.reference: Snowpark ML — to_sklearn() and Feature Importance

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to encode high-cardinality categorical features before training. Select TWO Snowpark ML preprocessing transformers appropriate for this scenario.

options.text: OrdinalEncoder — encodes categories as sequential integers, memory-efficient for high-cardinality features
OneHotEncoder with handle_unknown='ignore' — creates a binary column per category but can handle unseen categories at inference
StandardScaler — normalizes numeric columns and works for high-cardinality categoricals
MinMaxScaler — scale categorical codes to [0,1]

questions.explanation: A is correct: OrdinalEncoder maps each category to an integer, which is memory-efficient for high-cardinality features and works well with tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost) that can learn categorical splits. B is correct: OneHotEncoder with handle_unknown='ignore' creates binary indicators and gracefully handles categories unseen during training by zeroing out the unknown columns at inference time, preventing pipeline failures. C is wrong: StandardScaler operates on numeric columns; it cannot encode categorical strings. D is wrong: MinMaxScaler is also for numeric columns, not categorical encoding.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML Preprocessing — OrdinalEncoder, OneHotEncoder

questions.stem: A Snowpark ML Pipeline containing a StandardScaler and a LogisticRegression is trained with `pipeline.fit(train_df)`. A new batch of data arrives. What is the CORRECT way to preprocess AND predict in a single call?

options.text: pipeline.transform(new_batch_df) then pipeline.predict(transformed_df)
pipeline.predict(new_batch_df) — the Pipeline applies all preprocessing steps before calling the final estimator's predict()
scaler.transform(new_batch_df) then model.predict(scaled_df)
pipeline.fit(new_batch_df) then pipeline.predict(new_batch_df)

questions.explanation: B is correct: one of the core benefits of a Snowpark ML Pipeline is that calling predict() on the pipeline applies all upstream transformation steps (StandardScaler in this case) before passing the result to the final estimator. This is the whole point of the Pipeline abstraction. A is wrong: Pipeline.transform() applies only the transformers (not the final estimator), and you don't need to call both. C is wrong: this defeats the purpose of the pipeline and requires separate references to the scaler and model. D is wrong: calling fit() on the new batch would refit the scaler and model to the new data, not use the trained pipeline.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML Pipeline — predict() applying all steps

questions.stem: A data scientist uses `RandomizedSearchCV` from Snowpark ML with `n_iter=20` and `cv=5`. How many model fits will be executed?
questions.explanation: C is correct: RandomizedSearchCV samples n_iter=20 parameter combinations. For each combination, it fits the model cv=5 times (once per fold). Total fits = n_iter × cv = 20 × 5 = 100. A is wrong: 20 would only be correct with cv=1. B is wrong: 5 is just the number of folds per combination, not the total. D is wrong: 25 = 5 × 5 which doesn't match the computation.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML RandomizedSearchCV — n_iter × cv Computation

questions.stem: Which Snowpark ML class is used to build a classification model using gradient boosted trees from the LightGBM framework, executing inside Snowflake?

options.text: snowflake.ml.modeling.lightgbm.LGBMClassifier
lightgbm.LGBMClassifier
snowflake.ml.modeling.ensemble.GradientBoostingClassifier
snowflake.ml.modeling.xgboost.XGBClassifier

questions.explanation: A is correct: Snowpark ML provides a LightGBM wrapper in snowflake.ml.modeling.lightgbm module as LGBMClassifier, which executes inside Snowflake. B is wrong: lightgbm.LGBMClassifier is the vanilla LightGBM library that runs locally. C is wrong: GradientBoostingClassifier in the ensemble module is sklearn's implementation; it is available in Snowpark ML but is a different algorithm from LightGBM. D is wrong: XGBClassifier in the xgboost module is XGBoost, not LightGBM.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML — LightGBM Module

questions.stem: A data scientist has a fitted Snowpark ML Pipeline and needs to save it for later use. Select TWO valid approaches.

options.text: Log the fitted pipeline to the Snowflake ML Model Registry using registry.log_model(pipeline, ...)
Call pipeline.to_bytes() and store the result in a Snowflake table column
Use Python's joblib.dump() to save the pipeline to a local file, then upload to a Snowflake internal stage
Snowpark ML pipelines cannot be persisted and must be retrained each session

questions.explanation: A is correct: the Snowflake ML Model Registry accepts Snowpark ML Pipeline objects just like individual estimators; log_model() serializes and stores the complete pipeline. C is correct: Python's joblib serialization works for Snowpark ML models (they wrap sklearn-compatible objects). The serialized file can be uploaded to a Snowflake internal stage and later retrieved. B is wrong: there is no to_bytes() method on Snowpark ML Pipeline objects. D is wrong: pipelines can be persisted using both the Registry and standard Python serialization.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML Pipeline — Persistence Options

questions.stem:
A data scientist defines the following Snowpark ML model:

```python
from snowflake.ml.modeling.xgboost import XGBRegressor

model = XGBRegressor(
    input_cols=['F1','F2','F3'],
    label_cols=['PRICE'],
    output_cols=['PREDICTED_PRICE'],
    n_estimators=200,
    learning_rate=0.05
)
model.fit(train_df)
```

The training takes 45 minutes on an X-Small warehouse. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE single change to reduce training time?

options.text: Reduce n_estimators to 100
Increase the warehouse size to LARGE or X-LARGE
Convert train_df to pandas and train locally
Switch from XGBRegressor to LinearRegression

questions.explanation: B is correct: increasing the warehouse size provides significantly more compute (CPU, memory, and parallelism). An X-Small warehouse has 1 node; a LARGE warehouse has 8 nodes and 16x the concurrency. For Snowpark ML training on large datasets, warehouse scale-up is typically the most impactful lever. A is wrong: reducing n_estimators may reduce training time but also reduces model quality; it is not the 'most effective' architectural change. C is wrong: converting to pandas for local training re-introduces the data export bottleneck and was exactly what Snowpark ML was designed to avoid. D is wrong: switching algorithms is a correctness and model architecture decision, not a performance optimization.
questions.reference: Snowpark ML — Warehouse Sizing Optimization

questions.stem: How does a data scientist initialize a connection to the Snowflake Feature Store in Python?

options.text: fs = FeatureStore(session=session, database='ML_DB', name='FEATURE_STORE', default_warehouse='COMPUTE_WH')
fs = FeatureStore.connect(session)
fs = session.feature_store(name='FEATURE_STORE')
fs = FeatureStore(connection_string='snowflake://...')

questions.explanation: A is correct: the FeatureStore constructor requires a Snowpark session, a database name, the feature store name (schema), and a default warehouse for compute. B is wrong: there is no connect() class method on FeatureStore. C is wrong: there is no feature_store() method on the Snowpark session object. D is wrong: FeatureStore uses a Snowpark session, not a raw connection string.
questions.reference: Snowflake Feature Store — Initialization

questions.stem: A data scientist calls `fs.retrieve_feature_values(spine_df, feature_views=[fv1, fv2], spine_timestamp_col='EVENT_TS')`. The spine has 1 million rows. What does point-in-time correctness guarantee here?

options.text: Features from fv1 and fv2 are joined using only feature values that were available at or before the EVENT_TS for each spine row, preventing future data leakage
Only the most recent feature values are joined, regardless of spine timestamp
Feature values are joined based on approximate timestamp matching with a 1-minute tolerance
Point-in-time correctness only applies when a single Feature View is retrieved, not multiple

questions.explanation: A is correct: point-in-time correct joining ensures that for each observation in the spine DataFrame, only feature values that existed at or before the EVENT_TS are used. This prevents the model from seeing future information during training, which would cause data leakage and unrealistically inflated evaluation metrics. B is wrong: using only the most recent features would leak future data for historical training rows. C is wrong: there is no 1-minute tolerance; the join is exact as-of the timestamp. D is wrong: point-in-time correctness applies to all Feature Views in the feature_views list, not just single-view retrieval.
questions.reference: Snowflake Feature Store — Point-in-Time Correct Joins

questions.stem: A data scientist team reviews two Feature View options: one with a 15-minute refresh lag and one with a 24-hour lag. Select TWO scenarios where the 24-hour lag Feature View is PREFERRED.

options.text: A daily batch churn prediction model that uses 30-day rolling behavioral features
A real-time fraud detection model that requires features computed from the last 5 minutes of transactions
A weekly credit risk model trained on monthly aggregated features
A streaming recommendation system that needs up-to-the-minute feature values

questions.explanation: A is correct: a daily batch churn model using 30-day rolling features does not require fresh features every 15 minutes. A 24-hour refresh is perfectly adequate and will be more cost-effective. C is correct: a weekly credit risk model using monthly aggregated features has extremely low freshness requirements; 24-hour lag features are more than sufficient and the longer refresh interval reduces compute cost. B is wrong: a real-time fraud detection model requires very fresh features; 24-hour lag would mean severely stale data. D is wrong: a streaming recommendation system needs up-to-the-minute features, making 24-hour lag entirely unsuitable.
questions.reference: Snowflake Feature Store — Refresh Lag Design Decisions

questions.stem: A data scientist uses `fs.list_feature_views()` and wants to retrieve the underlying Snowpark DataFrame for a specific Feature View to inspect the features. Which method is correct?

options.text: fv.feature_df
fs.get_feature_view(name='customer_features', version='v1').to_df()
fs.query_feature_view(name='customer_features')
fv.read().to_df()

questions.explanation: B is correct: fs.get_feature_view(name, version) retrieves the FeatureView object from the Feature Store, and .to_df() returns a Snowpark DataFrame containing the materialized feature values from the underlying Dynamic Table or view. A is wrong: feature_df on the FeatureView object contains the source computation DataFrame, not the materialized features. C is wrong: query_feature_view() is not a FeatureStore method. D is wrong: read() is not a FeatureView method.
questions.reference: Snowflake Feature Store — get_feature_view() and to_df()

questions.stem: A Feature View is registered with two entities: 'customer' (join key: CUSTOMER_ID) and 'merchant' (join key: MERCHANT_ID). A spine DataFrame has both CUSTOMER_ID and MERCHANT_ID columns. How does `fs.retrieve_feature_values()` handle the join?

options.text: It joins on CUSTOMER_ID only; multi-entity Feature Views are not supported
It performs a compound join on both CUSTOMER_ID and MERCHANT_ID simultaneously, computing the intersection
It joins on each entity's join key separately and returns two separate result sets
It raises an error because a Feature View can only have one entity

questions.explanation: B is correct: Snowflake Feature Store supports multi-entity Feature Views. When retrieving, the spine must contain all join keys for all associated entities. The retrieval performs a compound join matching all entity join keys simultaneously, enabling cross-entity feature computation (e.g., customer-merchant interaction features). A is wrong: multi-entity Feature Views are supported. C is wrong: the join is compound (simultaneous), not separate. D is wrong: Feature Views can have multiple entities.
questions.reference: Snowflake Feature Store — Multi-Entity Feature Views

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to generate training data by joining a label spine with registered feature views. After calling `fs.retrieve_feature_values(...)`, what is the return type?

options.text: A pandas DataFrame with all spine rows and joined feature values
A Snowpark DataFrame that can be passed directly to a Snowpark ML estimator's fit() method
A Python dictionary mapping spine row IDs to feature value dictionaries
A Snowflake internal stage path containing a Parquet file

questions.explanation: B is correct: fs.retrieve_feature_values() returns a Snowpark DataFrame. This is a key design choice — the result can be passed directly to model.fit() or pipeline.fit() without any intermediate materialization or format conversion, keeping all computation inside Snowflake. A is wrong: the result is a Snowpark DataFrame, not a pandas DataFrame (though you can call .to_pandas() if needed). C is wrong: a Python dictionary is not the return type. D is wrong: the data is not written to a stage; it's a lazy Snowpark DataFrame query.
questions.reference: Snowflake Feature Store — retrieve_feature_values() Return Type

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model to the Registry and wants to tag it as the 'champion' model for production use. Which Registry API method accomplishes this?

options.text: registry.set_default_model('fraud_detector', alias='champion')
model_version.set_alias('champion')
model_version.tag(name='production', value='champion')
registry.promote(model_name='fraud_detector', version='v2', stage='production')

questions.explanation: B is correct: the ModelVersion object's set_alias() method assigns a named alias (like 'champion', 'challenger', 'production') to a specific version, enabling lifecycle management without changing version names. A is wrong: set_default_model() is not the correct method; alias management is done on model version objects. C is wrong: tag() is not a Registry method; aliases are set with set_alias(). D is wrong: promote() is not a Registry API method; there is no built-in staging workflow with promote().
questions.reference: Snowflake ML Model Registry — set_alias()

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a TensorFlow Keras model using `registry.log_model(keras_model, model_name='image_classifier', version_name='v1', sample_input_data=sample_df)`. What does `sample_input_data` enable?

options.text: It specifies the training data and causes the Registry to retrain the model
It allows the Registry to infer the model's input schema (column names and data types) and validate inputs at inference time
It sets the default input for the SQL ! invocation syntax
It is used to pre-warm the model in the warehouse for faster first prediction

questions.explanation: B is correct: sample_input_data is used by the Registry to automatically infer the model's expected input schema — column names and data types. This metadata is stored with the model and used to validate inputs at inference time, provide better error messages, and enable SQL UDF type checking. A is wrong: the Registry does not retrain models; sample_input_data is purely for schema inference. C is wrong: sample_input_data does not set a default SQL input. D is wrong: there is no pre-warming mechanism in the Registry based on sample_input_data.
questions.reference: Snowflake ML Model Registry — sample_input_data for Schema Inference

questions.stem: A team wants to implement a model comparison workflow in the Snowflake ML Model Registry to choose between v1 and v2 of a revenue forecasting model. Select TWO Registry features that support this.

options.text: Logging evaluation metrics with set_metric() on each version (e.g., RMSE, MAE) for direct comparison
Using set_alias() to tag v1 as 'incumbent' and v2 as 'challenger' to track their roles
The Registry's built-in A/B testing framework that automatically routes traffic between versions
Using SHOW MODELS in SQL to compare model sizes and pick the smaller one

questions.explanation: A is correct: set_metric() allows attaching numeric evaluation metrics (RMSE, MAE, R², etc.) to each model version. These metrics can be retrieved and compared programmatically to make a data-driven version selection decision. B is correct: set_alias() provides semantic labels that make the comparison workflow clear — 'incumbent' is the current production model and 'challenger' is the candidate for promotion. C is wrong: the ML Model Registry does not have a built-in A/B testing framework; traffic routing requires external infrastructure. D is wrong: SHOW MODELS lists models but does not expose evaluation metrics for comparison.
questions.reference: Snowflake ML Model Registry — Model Comparison Workflow

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with `conda_dependencies=['scikit-learn==1.3.0', 'xgboost==2.0.0']` when calling `registry.log_model()`. What does this parameter specify?

options.text: The Python packages that must be installed in the warehouse environment when running inference
The packages used to train the model on the data scientist's local machine
The packages to install in the data scientist's local Conda environment
Dependency declarations are optional metadata only; the Registry does not use them for execution

questions.explanation: A is correct: conda_dependencies specifies the Python packages (and their versions) required to run the model in Snowflake's warehouse environment during inference. The Registry uses this information to provision the correct runtime environment when model_ref.run() is called. B is wrong: while these may also be the training dependencies, the parameter's purpose is to define the execution environment for inference inside Snowflake. C is wrong: conda_dependencies configures the Snowflake-side environment, not the local developer environment. D is wrong: the Registry actively uses conda_dependencies to provision the inference runtime environment.
questions.reference: Snowflake ML Model Registry — conda_dependencies

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a custom PyFunc model to the Snowflake ML Model Registry. What must the custom model class implement to be callable from SQL?

options.text: A __call__() method with Snowpark DataFrame input/output signatures
A predict() method (and optionally other methods), with the class decorated or registered as a custom model in the Snowpark ML framework
A SQL stored procedure definition embedded in the Python class using @udf decorator
A from_pickle() class method for deserialization inside Snowflake

questions.explanation: B is correct: for the Snowflake ML Model Registry, a custom model must expose a predict() method (and optionally other methods like predict_proba()). The class should inherit from or be compatible with the Snowpark ML custom model API, which the Registry uses to generate the corresponding SQL UDF or stored procedure for the ! invocation syntax. A is wrong: __call__() is not the interface required by the Registry; predict() is. C is wrong: the @udf decorator is for standalone Snowpark UDFs, not for Registry custom models. D is wrong: from_pickle() is not a required interface for custom Registry models.
questions.reference: Snowflake ML Model Registry — Custom Model Interface

questions.stem:
A data scientist runs:

```sql
SELECT
  product_id,
  SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.TRANSLATE(description, 'fr', 'en') AS translated
FROM french_products;
```

What does the second argument 'fr' specify?

options.text: The target language for the translation
The source language of the input text
The language model to use for translation
The confidence threshold for the translation

questions.explanation: B is correct: the TRANSLATE function signature is TRANSLATE(text, source_language, target_language). The second argument 'fr' specifies French as the source language of the input text. The third argument 'en' specifies English as the target language. A is wrong: the target language is the third argument ('en'). C is wrong: there is no language model selection argument in TRANSLATE. D is wrong: there is no confidence threshold argument.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex — TRANSLATE Function Signature

questions.stem: A data scientist trains a SNOWFLAKE.ML.ANOMALY_DETECTION model using labeled data. The label column contains TRUE for known anomalies and FALSE for normal observations. After training and predicting, what columns appear in the output?

options.text: ANOMALY_SCORE only
TS (timestamp), SERIES (value), IS_ANOMALY (boolean), ANOMALY_SCORE (float), LOWER_BOUND, UPPER_BOUND
PREDICTED_LABEL and CONFIDENCE
SERIES, Z_SCORE, and OUTLIER_RANK

questions.explanation: B is correct: the ANOMALY_DETECTION prediction output includes the timestamp column, the original series values, IS_ANOMALY (a boolean flag), ANOMALY_SCORE (a numeric score indicating degree of anomaly), and LOWER_BOUND/UPPER_BOUND (the expected range). A is wrong: multiple columns are returned, not just ANOMALY_SCORE. C is wrong: PREDICTED_LABEL and CONFIDENCE are not the output column names. D is wrong: Z_SCORE and OUTLIER_RANK are not the anomaly detection output columns.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex ML — ML.ANOMALY_DETECTION Output Schema

questions.stem: A data scientist is deciding between SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.COMPLETE with a prompt and SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SUMMARIZE for a text summarization task. Select TWO situations where COMPLETE is preferred over SUMMARIZE.

options.text: When the summary must follow a specific format (e.g., exactly 3 bullet points)
When the task requires summarizing a single long document without any specific output constraints
When the summary must include a sentiment score alongside the summarized text
When minimizing token cost is the primary concern

questions.explanation: A is correct: COMPLETE with a carefully crafted prompt can enforce specific output formatting requirements like '3 bullet points' or 'maximum 50 words'. SUMMARIZE does not accept output format constraints. C is correct: COMPLETE can be prompted to produce multi-part outputs like 'summary: ... sentiment: ...' in a single call. SUMMARIZE only returns a summary text. B is wrong: for straightforward single-document summarization without constraints, SUMMARIZE is simpler and more appropriate. D is wrong: COMPLETE typically uses more tokens than SUMMARIZE for the same task because it requires a longer prompt; SUMMARIZE would be more cost-effective.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex — COMPLETE vs SUMMARIZE Use Cases

questions.stem: A data scientist uses `SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.COMPLETE` with a structured prompt to extract JSON from customer feedback. They observe that the model sometimes returns malformed JSON. What is the BEST mitigation strategy within Snowflake?

options.text: Switch from COMPLETE to EXTRACT_ANSWER which always returns valid JSON
Use COMPLETE with the structured output (JSON schema) variant that enforces a response schema, or use TRY_PARSE_JSON() in SQL to handle parse failures gracefully
Use SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.CLASSIFY_TEXT which is more reliable for structured output
There is no way to enforce structured output from COMPLETE; always parse responses externally

questions.explanation: B is correct: Snowflake Cortex COMPLETE supports a structured output variant where a JSON schema can be provided, constraining the model's output to valid JSON matching the schema. Additionally, TRY_PARSE_JSON() in SQL returns NULL instead of an error on malformed JSON, allowing graceful handling of failures. A is wrong: EXTRACT_ANSWER does not return JSON; it returns a text span. C is wrong: CLASSIFY_TEXT is for categorization tasks and does not produce structured JSON output for arbitrary fields. D is wrong: the structured output variant of COMPLETE does enforce JSON schemas.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex COMPLETE — Structured Output and TRY_PARSE_JSON

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to create a Cortex Search service over internal knowledge base articles. After creating the service, what is the primary way to query it from a Python application?

options.text: Using a SQL SELECT with a LIKE clause against the search service
Using the Cortex Search REST API or the Python SDK's CortexSearchService.search() method
Using SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.EXTRACT_ANSWER() pointed at the search service
Using SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SEARCH() as a SQL aggregate function

questions.explanation: B is correct: Cortex Search services are queried via the Cortex Search REST API or the Snowpark Python SDK's CortexSearchService object, which provides a search() method. This enables integration with application backends and chatbots. A is wrong: LIKE clause performs exact keyword pattern matching, not semantic search. C is wrong: EXTRACT_ANSWER is a function on document text, not a search mechanism over a search service. D is wrong: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SEARCH() is not a SQL aggregate function.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex Search — REST API and Python SDK

questions.stem: A data scientist trains a SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST model on weekly sales with external regressors (promotion flags and holiday indicators). How are external regressors specified during training?

options.text: They are automatically detected from extra columns in the training DataFrame beyond the timestamp and target
Using the EXOGENOUS_COLUMNS parameter in the SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST stored procedure call
External regressors are not supported in SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST
Using a separate SNOWFLAKE.ML.ADD_REGRESSOR() call after creating the forecast model

questions.explanation: A is correct: SNOWFLAKE.ML.FORECAST automatically recognizes additional columns in the training input beyond the timestamp and target value as external regressors (also called exogenous variables). These columns must also be provided for the future forecast horizon during prediction. B is wrong: there is no EXOGENOUS_COLUMNS parameter; extra columns are automatically used as regressors. C is wrong: external regressors are a supported feature of ML.FORECAST. D is wrong: there is no ADD_REGRESSOR() function; regressors are included as columns in the training data.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex ML — ML.FORECAST External Regressors

questions.stem: A data analyst uses `SNOWFLAKE.ML.TOP_INSIGHTS` to analyze a decline in customer conversion rate. Select TWO TRUE statements about the output of this function.

options.text: It outputs dimension-value combination slices ranked by their contribution to the metric change
It outputs raw transaction-level data with anomaly flags
Each output row includes a metric delta showing how much that slice contributed to the overall change
It only analyzes one dimension at a time and cannot handle multi-dimensional combinations

questions.explanation: A is correct: TOP_INSIGHTS produces output rows representing dimension slice combinations (e.g., REGION='North' AND CHANNEL='Mobile'), ranked by their contribution to the overall metric change between the baseline and current windows. C is correct: each output row includes a quantified metric delta showing how much that particular dimension slice contributed to the total change, enabling prioritization of investigation efforts. B is wrong: TOP_INSIGHTS analyzes aggregated slice-level metrics, not raw transaction-level data. D is wrong: TOP_INSIGHTS explicitly handles multi-dimensional combinations as a core feature.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex ML — ML.TOP_INSIGHTS Output Format

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to use Snowflake Arctic, Snowflake's own LLM, with the COMPLETE function. Which model identifier should they use?

options.text: 'arctic'
'snowflake-arctic'
'snowflake-arctic-instruct'
'snowflake/arctic-v1'

questions.explanation: C is correct: the model identifier for Snowflake's Arctic LLM in Cortex COMPLETE is 'snowflake-arctic-instruct'. Arctic is an enterprise-focused LLM optimized for instruction-following tasks. A is wrong: 'arctic' is not the correct identifier. B is wrong: 'snowflake-arctic' is not the correct identifier; the '-instruct' suffix is required for the instruction-tuned variant. D is wrong: 'snowflake/arctic-v1' is not the Cortex model identifier format.
questions.reference: Snowflake Cortex COMPLETE — Snowflake Arctic Model Identifier

questions.stem: A data scientist uses `df.describe()` on a Snowpark DataFrame and observes that a numeric column shows count=1000 while the total row count is 1500. What can they infer?

options.text: The column has 500 duplicate values
The column has 500 null values, since COUNT excludes NULLs
The column has 500 values below zero which were excluded
The describe() method only processes a 1000-row sample

questions.explanation: B is correct: in Snowflake (and pandas), COUNT() excludes NULL values. If the total row count is 1500 but COUNT shows 1000 for a column, there are 1500 - 1000 = 500 NULL values in that column. A is wrong: duplicate values are included in COUNT(); duplicates do not reduce the count. C is wrong: negative values are included in COUNT(); they do not cause exclusion. D is wrong: describe() processes the entire DataFrame, not a sample.
questions.reference: Snowpark DataFrame describe() — NULL Handling in COUNT

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to compute the Pearson correlation matrix for 50 numeric feature columns in Snowflake. What is the most efficient approach?

options.text: Collect all data to pandas with .to_pandas() and call df.corr()
Write a SQL query using CORR() aggregate for every pair of columns (50×49/2 = 1225 pairs) in a single query using conditional aggregation
Use the Snowflake CORRELATION_MATRIX() built-in function
Use Snowpark's df.stat.corr() to compute all pairwise correlations inside Snowflake without data export

questions.explanation: B is correct: while verbose, a single SQL query using CORR(col_i, col_j) for all pairs in one aggregation pass is the most compute-efficient approach since it requires only one full table scan. The result can then be pivoted or reshaped. A is wrong: collecting 50 feature columns × millions of rows to pandas may exhaust memory and defeats the Snowflake-native approach. C is wrong: CORRELATION_MATRIX() is not a built-in Snowflake SQL function. D is wrong: df.stat.corr() in Snowpark only accepts two columns and computes a single pairwise correlation, not a matrix across all 50 features automatically.
questions.reference: Snowflake SQL — CORR for Pairwise Correlation Matrix

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to sample 100 rows randomly from a very large Snowflake table using SQL without specifying an exact row count guarantee. Which SQL clause should they use?

options.text: SELECT * FROM large_table LIMIT 100
SELECT * FROM large_table SAMPLE (100 ROWS)
SELECT * FROM large_table SAMPLE (1 PERCENT)
SELECT * FROM large_table ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT 100

questions.explanation: B is correct: SAMPLE (100 ROWS) in Snowflake SQL uses Bernoulli or block-level sampling to select approximately 100 rows randomly. Unlike LIMIT, it returns rows from throughout the table rather than always from the start. A is wrong: LIMIT 100 returns the first 100 rows (based on storage order), which may not be representative. C is wrong: SAMPLE (1 PERCENT) samples by percentage, not by absolute row count — 1% of a 10M row table would be 100K rows. D is wrong: ORDER BY RANDOM() requires a full sort which is extremely expensive on large tables.
questions.reference: Snowflake SQL — SAMPLE Clause

questions.stem: A data scientist deploys a Streamlit in Snowflake app that calls a ML Model Registry model using the ! syntax. A junior analyst with only SELECT on the app tries to run the app. What access does the analyst need to also call the Registry model?

options.text: No additional access is needed; the app runs under the app owner's credentials
The analyst's role must have USAGE privilege on the Streamlit app; the model invocation runs under the analyst's own session role, which must have sufficient privileges on the model
The analyst must be granted ACCOUNTADMIN to invoke ML models from Streamlit
The analyst needs a separate API key to access the ML Model Registry

questions.explanation: B is correct: in Streamlit in Snowflake, the app executes under the calling user's session and role. The user needs USAGE on the Streamlit app to open it, and their active role must also have the necessary privileges to invoke the ML Registry model (typically, USAGE on the model object or its schema). A is wrong: Streamlit in Snowflake does not use app-owner credentials by default; it runs under the user's own session role. C is wrong: ACCOUNTADMIN is not required for model invocation; appropriate object-level privileges suffice. D is wrong: there is no separate API key for ML Model Registry access within Snowflake.
questions.reference: Streamlit in Snowflake — Access Control for ML Model Invocation

questions.stem: A data governance team wants to audit which users invoked ML Model Registry models over the past 30 days. Select TWO sources of audit information available in Snowflake.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — shows all queries including model invocations using the ! syntax
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY — shows object-level access including which users accessed model objects
The ML Model Registry's built-in invocation log table in the Registry schema
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ML_INVOCATION_LOG — a dedicated ML invocation audit view

questions.explanation: A is correct: QUERY_HISTORY in the ACCOUNT_USAGE schema records every query executed, including those using the model ! syntax for ML Model Registry invocations. Governance teams can filter by query text or user. B is correct: ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which objects (tables, functions, models) were accessed by which users and roles, providing a comprehensive audit trail at the object level. C is wrong: there is no built-in invocation log table automatically maintained in the Registry schema. D is wrong: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ML_INVOCATION_LOG is not a real Snowflake system view.
questions.reference: Snowflake Governance — QUERY_HISTORY and ACCESS_HISTORY for ML Audit
